skill.name: structured-logging
skill.description: Implement JSON-based structured logging for Node.js services, including log levels, request context, redaction, and log aggregation patterns.
skill.summary: Guides teams through practical JSON structured logging for observable services.
skill.author: Doyajin174

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported a critical dangerous-combination heuristic, but review found a documentation-only skill with markdown examples for logging libraries. The Ruby backtick, environment file, browser storage, certificate, and weak crypto hits are false positives caused by code fences, AsyncLocalStorage API names, redaction examples, and descriptive text. The remaining low risk is broad allowed tool access and copyable npm install examples, not confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Broad Tool Permissions for a Documentation Skill
low_findings.description: The skill declares Read, Glob, Grep, Edit, Write, and Bash access even though the content is a guide. This is not malicious by itself, but it expands the actions an assistant may take while following examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration is explicit and includes Bash plus file editing tools. The surrounding content is instructional, so the risk is permission breadth rather than confirmed abuse.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Examples Are Benign Configuration Reads
low_findings.description: The process.env references configure LOG_LEVEL and NODE_ENV in sample logger setup. No evidence found that secrets are read, logged, transmitted, or stored by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines use environment variables only for log level, runtime environment, and Datadog environment labels. This strongly supports a false positive for credential access.

low_findings.title: Network References Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded network values are a localhost Elasticsearch node and public documentation links. No evidence found of active network requests performed by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network indicators are visible in a sample integration block and references section. There is no hidden endpoint, encoded payload, or exfiltration pattern.

low_findings.title: Static Code Execution Detections Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick locations correspond to markdown code fences, inline code formatting, and TypeScript snippets. The only shell snippets install logging packages with npm.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review shows markdown fences and examples, not executable skill code. The skill contains no script file or command wrapper that would run these snippets automatically.

content.value_statement: Plain text logs are hard to search, filter, and connect across services. This skill helps Claude, Codex, or Claude Code design JSON logs with useful context, safe redaction, and aggregation-ready fields.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use JSON structured logs instead of plain text logs.
Defines common log levels and recommended production level behavior.
Provides Node.js examples for Pino, Winston, request IDs, and AsyncLocalStorage context.
Shows patterns for API request logs, business event logs, and performance logs.
Describes sensitive field redaction and examples for ELK and Datadog integration.
limitations: Examples focus on Node.js and TypeScript applications.
It does not provide a ready-made package or reusable logging module.
Aggregation guidance is introductory and does not replace vendor setup documentation.
The skill does not automatically audit existing logs for sensitive data.

use_cases.title: Add logging to a new API service
use_cases.description: Design consistent JSON fields, request IDs, and error logs before the service reaches production.

use_cases.title: Improve production troubleshooting
use_cases.description: Standardize logs so incidents can be searched by request, user, operation, duration, and error code.

use_cases.title: Prepare logs for observability platforms
use_cases.description: Adapt application logs for ingestion by ELK, Datadog, or similar log aggregation systems.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Logger
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a Node.js project
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the structured-logging skill to design a basic JSON logger for my Node.js service. Include log levels, default fields, and safe development output.

prompt_templates.title: Add Request Context
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the structured-logging skill to add request ID propagation and context-aware logging to my API middleware. Keep the output compatible with JSON log aggregation.

prompt_templates.title: Standardize Event Logs
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the structured-logging skill to define logging standards for authentication, payment, and database events. Include required fields and sensitive data rules.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Observability Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the structured-logging skill to review my service logging plan for ELK or Datadog ingestion. Identify missing context, redaction gaps, and operational checklist items.

output_examples.input: A developer asks for a logging standard for a Node.js API.
output: A recommended logger configuration with service and environment fields.
A request logging pattern with request ID, method, status, duration, and error details.
A short checklist for redaction, log levels, and aggregation readiness.

output_examples.input: An SRE asks why production logs are hard to search during incidents.
output: A diagnosis of missing structured fields and inconsistent messages.
A proposed field set for request, user, operation, duration, and error code.
Guidance for using log levels without excessive debug noise.

output_examples.input: A platform engineer asks how to prepare logs for Datadog tracing.
output: A plan to include service, environment, trace ID, and span ID in log events.
A note to redact authorization headers, tokens, passwords, and payment data.
A rollout checklist for development, staging, and production validation.

best_practices: Define a stable field schema before adding logs across many services.
Redact tokens, passwords, payment data, and authorization headers before logs leave the application.
Use request IDs and operation names so related events can be searched together.
anti_patterns: Do not log secrets, full payment details, or raw authorization headers.
Do not replace structured fields with interpolated text messages.
Do not enable high-volume debug or trace logs in production by default.

faq.question: What problem does this skill solve?
faq.answer: It helps teams replace plain text logs with searchable JSON logs that carry request, user, service, and error context.

faq.question: Which languages does it cover best?
faq.answer: The examples focus on Node.js and TypeScript, especially Pino and Winston.

faq.question: Does it send logs to an external service?
faq.answer: No. It explains integration patterns, but the skill itself does not transmit logs or run a collector.

faq.question: Can it help prevent secret leaks in logs?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes redaction guidance and warns against logging passwords, tokens, payment data, and authorization headers.

faq.question: Is this useful without ELK or Datadog?
faq.answer: Yes. Structured logs improve local debugging, production search, and future migration to log aggregation platforms.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk level low?
faq.answer: The scanner flagged markdown examples as risky code, but review found no active malicious behavior. Broad tool permissions remain a minor concern.
